content:
What looked like another AI standard became the missing layer between models, tools, and production systems.

5 min read
Just now

--

We were not shipping features anymore. We were maintaining integrations.
Slack broke. Jira changed an API without warning. Someone rotated an OAuth token and forgot to tell the team. Our AI agent was not getting smarter. It was getting harder to keep alive.
I remember sitting in a sprint retro defending a Jira adapter that had broken for the third time that month. I called it an edge case. It was the fourth edge case that quarter, and I already knew that when I said it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

That is the moment I actually became an MCP skeptic.
Every vendor promising “the standard that connects everything” eventually becomes one more thing engineers have to maintain forever.
The Fixes That Did Not Work
So we tried the obvious fixes first.
We wrote a shared retry wrapper, hoping it would absorb the flakiness instead of each adapter handling failures on its own. It helped for about a month, until two adapters needed different backoff timing and the shared wrapper became yet another thing…
